content.value: What are the networks available to Docker containers?
Docker uses network drivers to manage container communication. Below are the primary network types available in Docker:
1. None Network
Used when your container doesn’t provide a service over the network and requires complete network isolation.

items: The container isn’t provided with a network interface or an IP address.
Within the container, only the loopback device (localhost) is created.
Maximum security and isolation from all external and internal traffic.

content.value: docker run -d --network none --name testApp nginx
content.label: Run container with no network

content.value: 2. Host Network
Used when you would like to use the Docker host’s network stack directly, removing isolation between the host and the container.

items: Services running on any port within your container will be directly accessible through the host’s IP.
Performance: Offers better performance as it avoids the Docker NAT (Network Address Translation).
Note: Published ports (-p option) are discarded and do not work in host mode.

content.value: docker run -d --network host --name nginx_on_host nginx
content.label: Run container on Host network

content.value: 3. Bridge Network (Default)
This is the default network driver. If you don't specify a driver, this is the one used.

items: Used for standalone containers that need to communicate.
Containers on the default bridge can communicate via IP addresses.
Needs “linking” (--link) for legacy container DNS resolution (deprecated in favor of user-defined bridges).

content.value:
docker run -d --network bridge --name nginx_bridge nginx
docker run -d --network bridge -p 8080:80 --name nginx_on_bridge_with_port nginx
content.label: Using the Default Bridge

content.value: 4. User-Defined Bridge
Recommended for production. It provides better isolation and interoperability than the default bridge.

items: Used when you have a requirement for multiple networks on your Docker host.
Allows you to isolate different deployments of containers on the same host.
Provides automatic DNS resolution between containers by name.

content.value:
docker network create --driver bridge isolated_nw01
docker run -d --network isolated_nw01 -p 8080:80 --name nginx_ud_bridge nginx
content.label: Creating and using a User-Defined Bridge

content.value: 5. Overlay Network (Swarm)
Used for Multihost networking across Docker hosts that are part of a Swarm cluster.

items: Enables containers on different physical or virtual hosts to talk to each other as if they were on the same network.
Handles routing and encryption of traffic between nodes automatically.

content.value: 6. Overlay (External Key-Value)
Used for multihost networking without Swarm, requiring an external key-value store (like Consul, Etcd, or ZooKeeper).
To configure, modify the Docker service file (/lib/systemd/system/docker.service):
-H tcp://0.0.0.0:2375 -H unix:///var/run/docker.sock --cluster-store=consul://<consul_IP>:8500 --cluster-advertise=<node_IP>:2385
content.label: Docker Service Configuration

content.value: Reload the configuration:
systemctl daemon-reload
content.label: Reload Systemd

content.value: You can now create and use overlay networks across nodes:

# On all nodes:
docker network create --driver overlay mhn01

# Node 1:
docker run -itd --network mhn01 --name=container1 busybox

# Node 2:
docker run -itd --network mhn01 --name=container2 busybox
content.label: Cross-Node Communication

content.value: 7. Macvlan Network
Macvlan networks allow you to assign a MAC address to a container, making it appear as a physical device on your network.

items: Used for legacy applications that need to be directly connected to the physical network.
Traffic is routed by the Docker daemon to the container based on its MAC address.
Requires the host network interface to be in promiscuous mode.

content.value:
docker network create -d macvlan \
  --subnet=192.168.1.0/24 \
  --gateway=192.168.1.1 \
  -o parent=eth0 pub_net
content.label: Creating a Macvlan network

content.value: 8. Custom Network Plugins
If the built-in networking options do not fit your specific requirements, Docker allows you to use or write your own network plugins using the Docker Plugin API.

items: Third-party Plugins: Popular options include Weave, Calico, Cilium, and Kuryr.
Flexibility: Custom plugins can provide features like advanced security policies, multi-cloud connectivity, and specialized load balancing.

content.value: Resources & Documentation

items: Docker Plugin API: Extending Docker with Network Plugins
Sample Plugin: Introducing Weave Net
Developer Guide: Help with Writing Plugins

content.title: Describe different types of networks available for docker and containers.

items: None: No network interface or IP address is provided to the container. Useful when the container doesn't need to provide a service over the network.
Host: The container uses the Docker host's network stack, making services running on any port within the container directly accessible through the host's IP.
Bridge (default): Containers are connected to a private internal network on the Docker host. This is the default network type and requires linking for container DNS resolution.
Bridge (user-defined): Allows for multiple networks on the Docker host, useful for isolating different deployments of containers.
Overlay: An overlay network uses software virtualization to create additional layers of network abstraction running on top of a physical network. Used for multi-host network communication.
Overlay (swarm): Enables multi-host networking across Docker hosts that are part of a swarm.
Overlay (external key-value mechanism): Similar to the swarm overlay but uses an external key-value store for cluster management.
Custom network plugin: If the existing networking options don't fit your needs, you can write your own network plugin using the Docker plugin API.
